1: Psalms 9 and 10 form a single acrostic poem in Hebrew — successive stanzas begin with successive letters of the alphabet (aleph through taw). The LXX split them into two psalms and most Western translations follow that division, but the Hebrew Psalter (MT) treats them as one. The acrostic form is a wisdom device: completing the alphabet signals total coverage of the subject from A to Z. The superscription ʿal-mût labbēn ("to Muth-labben") is obscure — likely a tune name or cultic performance direction; no consensus exists on its meaning.
2: The verbs of praise cluster in vv. 1-2: give thanks, declare, rejoice, be glad, sing. This rhetorical multiplication of praise-terms is characteristic of the Psalter's hymnic register and signals a formal song of thanksgiving following a military or national deliverance. "O Most High" (ʿElyôn) is a divine epithet shared with Canaanite religion (El Elyon, attested at Ugarit) that Israel claimed for YHWH alone — it emphasizes sovereignty over all peoples, not only Israel.
3: The turning back of enemies "before your face" (millĕpānêkā) signals divine presence driving them out — the same language used of Israel's conquest in Deuteronomy (1:30; 9:3). In ANE victory accounts, the flight of enemies at the divine presence was the standard evidence of divine warfare. The stumbling and perishing of enemies frames the following section on divine judgment over the nations.
4: "You sat on the throne, judging with righteousness" — the image of God enthroned as judge corresponds to the divine council imagery of the ANE, where the high god presides over lesser deities and adjudicates human disputes. In Israelite theology, YHWH alone occupies this throne (cf. Ps 82). The psalmist's personal vindication ("my right and my cause") merges with cosmic divine justice — the individual's case is heard by the judge of all the earth.
5: The nations (gôyim) are the standard foil in Davidic psalms — the enemies of Israel's king are also enemies of YHWH's order. "Blotting out their name forever" echoes the destruction of Amalek (Exod 17:14; Deut 25:19) and uses the ANE idiom for total eradication: to destroy a name is to destroy memory, lineage, and legacy. Name-survival was the ancient equivalent of ongoing existence in the community's collective memory.
6: This verse shifts to direct address of the enemy. "Perpetual ruin" (šōʾôt leneṣaḥ) — šōʾāh is the same root used in post-biblical Hebrew for the Holocaust (Shoah), meaning desolation, ruin. The uprooting of cities evokes conquest imagery. "Their memory has perished" repeats the name-blotting theme of v. 5: in ANE culture, one's legacy resided in name and descendants, so erasure of memory was the ultimate defeat.
7: "But the LORD sits enthroned forever" — the contrast with v. 6 (enemy's end vs. God's eternal throne) is the theological hinge of the poem. Yāšab lĕʿôlām echoes the enthronement psalms (Pss 29, 47, 93, 96-99) in which YHWH's eternal kingship is proclaimed. In Second Temple worship this throne language functioned as a counter-claim against the pretensions of successive empires (Assyrian, Babylonian, Persian, Greek).
8: Universal judgment: "He judges the world (tēbēl) with righteousness; he rules the peoples with equity (mêšārîm)." Tēbēl is the inhabited world — all nations, not only Israel. Mêšārîm (equity, uprightness) is the opposite of judicial corruption. In the ANE world where gods were tribal and their justice parochial, the claim that Israel's God is the impartial judge of all peoples was a radical theological claim.
9: "The LORD is a refuge for the oppressed (dak), a stronghold in times of trouble." Dak means crushed, ground down — one broken by circumstances or oppressors. This verse is a thesis statement for the entire Pss 9-10 meditation. The image of YHWH as stronghold (miśgāb — high tower, inaccessible height) is the standard metaphor for royal protection of the vulnerable in both biblical and ANE tradition: the king builds strongholds in which the weak can shelter.
10: "Those who know your name will trust in you" — in Hebrew, "knowing the name" is not mere cognitive familiarity but covenantal experience of the person. YHWH's name carries the history of his faithfulness (Exod 3:14-15; 34:5-7). "You have never abandoned those who seek you" grounds the call to trust in historical testimony. Dāraš (seek) is the standard word for earnest inquiry directed at God — the same root as seeking God's face in the sanctuary (Ps 27:8).
11: "Sing praises to the LORD who dwells in Zion; declare his deeds among the nations" — Zion as the center of universal proclamation is central to the Zion theology developed in Isaiah and Micah (Isa 2:2-4; Mic 4:1-4). Second Temple Judaism viewed Zion as the point from which YHWH's Torah and justice would radiate outward to all peoples. The call to "declare his deeds among the nations" anticipates the missionary dimension developed more fully in Isaiah's Servant Songs.
12: "The one who avenges bloodshed (dāmîm)" — blood-vengeance was a defined social institution in ancient Israel. The gōʾēl haddām (avenger of blood) was a kinsman obligated to pursue the killer of a family member (Num 35; Deut 19). The psalmist applies this kin-redeemer role to God himself: YHWH is the ultimate avenger who does not forget the cry of the ʿănāwîm (humble, poor). The humble in the Psalter are those without a human advocate who therefore rely entirely on divine advocacy.
13: "Be gracious to me" (ḥānnēnî) — the standard petition formula in lament psalms. The shift from praise (vv. 1-12) to personal petition (v. 13) is characteristic of the Psalms' movement from the general to the particular, from God's cosmic victories to one's own suffering. "You who lift me back from the gates of death" — the gates of Sheol were a concrete spatial image; to be near death was to be at the threshold of the underworld domain (cf. Isa 38:10; Job 38:17).
14: "The gates of daughter Zion" — bat-ṣiyyôn (daughter Zion) is a personification of Jerusalem as a young woman, a figure appearing frequently in the Prophets (Isa 1:8; 16:1; Mic 4:8; Lam 1:6). Gates in ancient cities were the civic center — where courts convened, commerce happened, and public announcements were made (Ruth 4:1-11; Prov 31:23; Amos 5:12, 15). To declare praises "in the gates" is to make the proclamation as public and official as possible.
15: "The nations have sunk in the pit they made" — the retributive justice motif: the trap set for the righteous catches the wicked themselves (cf. Ps 7:15-16; Prov 26:27; Eccl 10:8). This boomerang pattern is a major wisdom theme in the Psalter and in Proverbs. The "concealed net" (rešet tāmĕnû) likely refers to the literal hunting trap — a net staked in the ground and covered with leaves to catch animals, used metaphorically for hidden schemes.
16: Higgaion Selah — two enigmatic liturgical terms. Higgaion (related to hāgāh, to murmur/meditate) possibly indicates a meditative interlude or a specific musical instruction. Selah appears 71 times in the Psalter and 3 times in Habakkuk — likely a liturgical pause for reflection or a musical rest, though its exact function is debated. "Ensnared by the work of their own hands" repeats the retributive pattern of v. 15 with the additional irony of self-entrapment.
17: "The wicked will return to Sheol (šĕʾôl), all the nations that forget God." Sheol in the Hebrew Bible is the realm of the dead — not a place of punishment in the later developed sense but the underworld destination of all who die, the land of shadows and silence (Job 10:21-22; Isa 14:9-11). The claim here is that those who "forget God" — who live as if divine governance does not exist — will return to that realm. The verb yāšûbû implies they originate there, a creation-ex-nihilo motif.
18: "The needy (ʾebyôn) will not always be forgotten" — the Psalter's vocabulary of poverty is nuanced: ʾebyôn (economically needy), ʿānî (oppressed, afflicted), dallîm (weak, lowly), ʿănāwîm (humble, meek). These categories overlap but carry distinct social implications. Second Temple covenant ethics placed particular responsibility on the community to protect these groups (Deut 15:11; Isa 58:6-7). The promise that hope will not "perish forever" directly counters the wicked's permanent ruin (v. 6).
19: "Rise up, O LORD! Let not man have his way; let the nations be judged in your presence!" — the imperative qûmāh (rise up) is a standard battle-cry petition (Num 10:35; Pss 3:7; 7:6; 10:12; 17:13). "Man" (ʾĕnôš — frail, mortal humanity) in contrast to God deflates imperial pretension; the same contrast appears in Isa 31:3 ("The Egyptians are human, not God"). To be judged "in your presence" (ʿal-pānêkā) is to stand before the divine court — exactly what the wicked have denied would happen (Ps 10:4, 11, 13).
20: "Strike them with fear, O LORD" (šîtāh yhwh môrāh) — môrāh is the terror induced by divine presence and action; not psychological fear but the overwhelming awe that accompanies theophany. The goal is recognition: "let the nations know they are only human" (ʾĕnôš). Selah closes the psalm (the first half of the 9-10 acrostic) with a liturgical pause, inviting the congregation to absorb the contrast between divine sovereignty and human frailty that the entire poem has established.
